summary: Gruber reviews the September 2006 iPod lineup refresh, working through each tier: Shuffle, Nano, and full-size iPod. He confesses his initial skepticism about the original Shuffle was wrong and declares it his favorite iPod, praising the new clip-based redesign. The new Nanos combine the best of the Mini and original Nano, while the updated full-size iPods deliver the battery life improvements essential for Apple's move into movie sales. He argues that dismissing current iPods as not 'real' video players is as absurd as saying the original 5 GB iPod wasn't a real music player. He closes by advocating for open game APIs on the iPod and musing that Apple's phone ambitions might simply be an iPod with phone features.
key_points: The iPod Shuffle alone generated roughly as much revenue as the entire iTunes Store, illustrating that Apple's hardware business dwarfs its content business in profitability.
Battery life was the essential upgrade for the full-size iPod — the jump from 2 to 3.5 hours of video playback makes movie watching viable and validates Apple's push into film sales.
Calling current iPods not 'real' video players because of screen size is as wrongheaded as saying the original 5 GB iPod wasn't a real music player — capabilities grow incrementally.
Apple's closed game APIs represent a missed opportunity; games should follow the music and video precedent where the iTunes Store competes on convenience rather than exclusivity.
Portable electronics are converging from different directions, and Apple's phone entry could simply be an iPod with phone features rather than an entirely new product line.
money_quotes: It ends up that the shuffle mode's randomizer does a better job picking music I want to hear than I do.

The iPod Shuffle epitomizes the appeal of Apple Computer — I can't think of any other gadget company that would make something this simple or put this much design effort into something so inexpensive.
It never ceases to amaze me just how horrible the buttons feel on so many gadgets; cell phone buttons, for example, are usually all mushy and wiggly, like loose baby teeth.
Claiming that these iPods aren't 'real' video players because their screens are too small is like saying the original iPods weren't 'real' audio players because they could only hold 5 GB.
Your eyes will burn out before the battery does on the 80 GB.
Let the games from the iTunes Store compete with the free market in terms of convenience, price, and quality, just like how the music and video from the iTunes Store do.
Instead of thinking of Apple's hypothetical entry into the mobile phone market as an entirely new 'iPhone' product line, couldn't it just be a new 'iPod Phone' — an iPod with phone features?
key_insight: Apple's genius with the iPod line is putting extraordinary design effort into even its cheapest products, while incrementally expanding each tier's capabilities — a strategy that makes dismissing any current iPod as not yet 'real' a fundamental misunderstanding of how Apple iterates.
